narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 40.3% chance that you would fail—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, LSAT score of 30, Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings of 2 and 1 respectively, middle family income bracket, and full-time enrollment plans. Among students who passed the bar, the average LSAT is 37.27 and Year 3 deciles average 5.91, giving context to how your profile stacks up. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your undergraduate GPA, your Year 3 decile ranking, and your LSAT score, in that order of influence. Your solid undergraduate GPA stood out positively, as it's higher than the 3.24 average for bar passers, and it significantly pulled the prediction toward expecting you to succeed. In contrast, your low Year 3 decile ranking pushed strongly in the opposite direction, signaling to the model a higher risk of bar failure compared to typical successful students. Your LSAT score also worked in your favor to some degree, nudging the odds toward passing, though not enough to offset the other concerns. Overall, while your background as a full-time student from a middle-income family aligns with many who succeed (93% of bar passers are full-time), the model weighed your later academic deciles heavily against you relative to admitted peers. This isn't a judgment on your potential, but a data-driven flag based on patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the typical 37+ for passers, and focus on strengthening your upper-year performance through study habits or tutoring that could improve future decile rankings. You might also explore programs with bar passage support or gather letters highlighting your resilience. With targeted improvements, you can reshape this profile for stronger future applications.
